# Science & Discovery — 1777

Scientific advances and explorations of 1777.

<!-- BEGIN GENERATED: category-index — maintained by build-structure; do not edit by hand -->
| Topic | Description |
|---|---|
| [Coulomb's Torsion Balance and Electrostatics](coulomb-electrostatics.md) | Charles-Augustin de Coulomb's 1777 prize memoir on the magnetic compass introduced the torsion balance, the precision instrument that by 1785 enabled the quantitative inverse-square law of electrostatic force now known as Coulomb's Law. |
| [Lavoisier's Oxygen Theory](lavoisier-oxygen-theory.md) | Antoine Lavoisier's 1777 memoir "On Combustion in General" attacked phlogiston theory with quantitative experiments, establishing combustion as chemical combination with oxygen and launching the Chemical Revolution. |
| [Royal Society Lightning Conductor Controversy](royal-society-lightning-controversy-1777.md) | An 1777 dispute over pointed versus rounded lightning conductors that became a proxy for Revolutionary War politics, forcing Royal Society president Sir John Pringle to resign for defending Franklin's design. |
| [William Herschel's Telescopes and Deep-Sky Observations](herschel-telescopes-deep-sky.md) | William Herschel established his Bath workshop and systematic sky-survey programme in 1777, hand-grinding precision reflectors whose optical quality enabled his 1781 discovery of Uranus, the first planet found in the telescopic era. |
<!-- END GENERATED: category-index -->
